name: Netherlands
summary: One of the strongest tech destinations on paper — world-class English usability, deep AI/tech market (ASML, Booking, Adyen), the 30% (→27%) expat tax break, a flagship salary-only Highly Skilled Migrant work route filed offshore from Dhaka, a fully open orientation-year permit, unrestricted spouse work and top universities (TU Delft, Eindhoven). The catch sits in citizenship, not work: Dutch naturalisation generally requires renouncing prior nationality and a Bangladeshi skilled migrant doesn't fit the narrow exemptions, so indefinite Dutch/EU long-term residence (keeping the BD passport) is the default and naturalisation an optional final step. Compounded by Wilders/PVV's extreme anti-Islam politics (proposed Quran ban, 'hijab tax', mosque closures), a tiny Bangladeshi community and high housing/tuition costs.

job-market.summary: A deep, English-operating tech and AI market across multiple hubs (ASML/Eindhoven, Booking, Adyen) — a top-tier European AI cluster with high pay boosted by the 30% ruling and easy capacity for two tech earners; strong international-graduate retention.

pros.text: Deep tech/AI market — ASML and the Eindhoven Brainport, Booking, Adyen — with AI engineers at €72k–150k+ (Amsterdam €85k–175k) and the 30% ruling lifting take-home pay.
The Highly Skilled Migrant scheme via recognised sponsors is fast and low-friction, and English operates fully in tech workplaces.

cons.text: Junior software pay (~€50k–58k) is moderate before the 30% ruling, and competition for entry roles is strong.

links.title: Zen van Riel — AI engineer salary, Netherlands/Amsterdam (2026)

visa-access.summary: A reliable recognised-sponsor route with an embassy and VFS in Dhaka — the university applies for the entry visa (MVV) and residence permit together, making student access relatively smooth and predictable, with no Bangladesh-specific ban.

pros.text: Dutch universities are recognised sponsors that apply for the student's entry visa (MVV) and residence permit together, a streamlined process; the Netherlands has an embassy and VFS in Dhaka.
No Bangladesh-specific visa ban.

cons.text: Proof of high tuition plus living funds is required, and the political climate is hardening on migration.

citizenship.summary: The decisive failure: Dutch naturalisation generally requires renouncing prior nationality, and a Bangladeshi skilled migrant doesn't fit the narrow exemptions — so both spouses cannot retain BD nationality via the standard route.

pros.text: Naturalisation is reachable after 5 years, and the Dutch passport ranks joint 4th on Henley 2026 (~185 destinations).

cons.text: Dutch nationality law generally requires renouncing your original nationality on naturalisation, and the exemptions (spouse of a Dutch national, refugee, born in NL) don't fit a Bangladeshi skilled migrant — so naturalising would forfeit BD nationality. Workaround: hold the indefinite Dutch/EU long-term permanent-residence permit (granted at 5 years, keeping the BD passport) and treat the renunciation-bearing citizenship as an optional final step.
Naturalisation also requires the Dutch civic-integration exam (A2 Dutch plus knowledge of society).

post-study-work.summary: The orientation year (zoekjaar) gives graduates 12 months of fully unrestricted work (no permit, no salary threshold), with a reduced Highly Skilled Migrant threshold afterwards making the offer-based conversion to skilled work near-deterministic — and each spouse independently holds their own orientation year.

pros.text: The orientation-year permit lets graduates work any job full-time with no work-permit restriction and no salary threshold for the year; it is open to Dutch and top-200 global graduates.
Afterwards a reduced Highly Skilled Migrant salary threshold applies, and each graduating spouse can hold their own orientation year.

links.title: The Hague International Centre — orientation year permit

spouse-family.summary: A standout: the partner of a Highly Skilled Migrant gets fully unrestricted work rights, family migration is straightforward, and children attend school free.

pros.text: The partner of a Highly Skilled Migrant receives an unrestricted work permit (any employer, no separate authorisation) — among the best spouse-work regimes anywhere.
Family migration for HSM holders is easy, and children attend school free; if both spouses study/work, each holds their own track.

cons.text: During the student phase, dependent-spouse work is more limited than on the HSM route.

links.title: IND — family member of a highly skilled migrant (official)

msc-study.summary: World-class English-taught CS/AI (TU Delft, Eindhoven, UvA, Leiden) with 2,100+ English programmes, but non-EU tuition is high (~€22k–26k) and student work is restricted.

pros.text: Top-tier English-taught CS/AI (TU Delft, TU Eindhoven, UvA, Leiden) within 2,100+ English programmes; a master qualifies for the orientation year.

cons.text: Non-EU tuition is high (TU Delft ~€25,633; Leiden CS+AI ~€22,500 for 2026/27), and non-EU student work is restricted (≈16 hrs/week with a work permit).

links.title: Mastersportal — AI master's in the Netherlands (2026)

pr-pathway.summary: Permanent residence/EU long-term status is reachable on a clear route after 5 years with the Highly Skilled Migrant route — ICT salaries comfortably clear the bar — requiring A2 Dutch civic integration.

pros.text: Permanent residence (or EU long-term residence) is reachable after 5 years, with the Highly Skilled Migrant route building the time and ICT salaries clearing the bar.

cons.text: PR requires passing the civic-integration exam (A2 Dutch), and the PVV-driven climate has made migration policy less predictable.

income-cost.summary: High salaries plus the 30% ruling give strong net pay, but Amsterdam's housing shortage and high tuition squeeze the budget.

pros.text: High tech salaries plus the 30% (→27%) tax-free ruling produce a strong net surplus and easy remittance.

cons.text: Amsterdam/Randstad housing is in severe shortage with high rents, and non-EU tuition is steep — squeezing the study phase and early career.

healthcare.summary: High-quality care via mandatory private basic insurance (~€140/month) for residents/workers; students typically use international insurance — universal but premium-based.

pros.text: High-quality care: residents/workers must take mandatory basic insurance (basisverzekering, ~€140/month) with broad coverage, and the welfare net is solid.

cons.text: Coverage is premium-based with an annual deductible (~€385), and students generally rely on international/private insurance before becoming insurable.

culture-language.summary: Among the world's best English usability — daily life and work run fully in English — though Dutch A2 is needed for status and the political tone has turned hostile.

pros.text: The Netherlands has among the highest English proficiency in the world — daily life, services and work are fully workable in English, with excellent livability (cycling, work-life balance).

cons.text: Dutch at A2 is required for PR and citizenship (civic integration), and the PVV-led political tone has cooled social acceptance for visible Muslims.

safety-law.summary: Very safe with top-tier rule of law (WJP ~9th), tempered by a sharply anti-Muslim political climate.

pros.text: Very low crime, top-tier rule of law (WJP ~9th) and strong civil justice and women's safety.

cons.text: The Wilders/PVV-driven climate has elevated anti-Muslim rhetoric and incidents, denting lived safety for visible minorities.

links.title: World Justice Project — Rule of Law Index 2025

politics.summary: The most explicitly anti-Islam politics in the set: Geert Wilders's PVV won the 2023 election (the 2024 coalition collapsed in 2025), campaigning to ban the Quran, tax the hijab and close mosques.

pros.text: Skilled-migration channels (Highly Skilled Migrant, orientation year) remain operative and economically valued.

cons.text: Geert Wilders's PVV won the 2023 election and remains the dominant force; it has proposed banning the Quran, a 'head-rag tax' on the hijab and closing mosques and Islamic schools — extreme state-level targeting of Muslims.
The 2024 coalition collapsed in mid-2025, adding policy instability.

links.title: TRT World — Wilders and the demonisation of Muslims

tax.summary: High headline tax (to 49.5%) substantially offset by the 30% (→27% from 2027) expat ruling, with a Netherlands–Bangladesh tax treaty in force.

pros.text: The 30% ruling (reducing to 27% from 2027) makes a large share of salary tax-free for up to 5 years for qualifying skilled migrants, and a Netherlands–Bangladesh double-taxation treaty supports repatriation.

cons.text: Headline income tax reaches 49.5%, and the ruling's qualifying salary thresholds and percentage are being tightened.

muslim-diaspora.summary: A ~5.8% Muslim population with ~400 mosques and abundant urban halal, but a negligible Bangladeshi community and a politically hostile (PVV) climate.

pros.text: Muslims are ~5.8% (~1m) with ~400 mosques and abundant halal in Amsterdam, Rotterdam, The Hague and Utrecht; a Surinamese South-Asian (Hindustani) community adds a cultural ecosystem.

cons.text: The Bangladeshi-specific community is tiny (~3,500), and the PVV's anti-Islam programme (Quran ban, hijab tax, mosque closures) makes the sentiment climate hostile.

links.title: Minority Rights Group — Muslims in the Netherlands

direct-work-route.summary: A flagship direct-work entry, tempered by a tightening trajectory. The Highly Skilled Migrant (kennismigrant) scheme is salary-only — no labour-market test, no points — with 2026 thresholds (€5,942/mo at 30+, €4,357 under-30) that an IND recognised sponsor files together with the MVV in one offshore procedure (≤2-week target) while the couple are still in Dhaka; the EU Blue Card is a parallel option. HSM → permanent residence and naturalisation both at 5 years; Amsterdam/Eindhoven tech demand plus the (shrinking) 30→27% expat ruling. Headwinds: a 2025 government proposal to raise the under-30 salary floor and tighten sponsor rules (not yet enacted) and a generally restrictive direction. The citizenship step forces renunciation (no BD-fitting exemption), so indefinite PR is the BD-passport-retaining default.

pros.text: Highly Skilled Migrant scheme is salary-only (no labour-market test), filed offshore from Dhaka by a recognised sponsor with a ~2-week decision; clean HSM → PR/citizenship ladder at 5 years.
Deep Amsterdam/Eindhoven tech demand and a mature recognised-sponsor regime; the 30% (→27% from 2027) expat tax facility offsets the high salary floor.

cons.text: High under-30 salary floor (€4,357/mo in 2026) and a 2025 government proposal to raise it further and tighten sponsor rules (not yet enacted); thin BD-specific track record.
Naturalisation forces renunciation of BD nationality (no fitting exemption) — indefinite Dutch/EU long-term residence is the BD-passport-retaining default.

links.title: Government to tighten up highly skilled migrant scheme (4 Jul 2025) — Government.nl (official)
